title: Reward biases spontaneous neural reactivation during sleep
description: This dataset comprises neuroimaging and electrophysiological recordings from 18 participants who performed reward-based cognitive tasks (FACE and MAZE games) during wakefulness in a 3T MRI scanner, followed by a sleep session with simultaneous EEG-fMRI acquisition. The study investigates how reward biases spontaneous neural reactivation during sleep by applying decoding classifiers trained on task-evoked activity to sleep-stage-dependent brain activity patterns. Participants underwent subsequent memory testing to assess task retention.
methods_description: Data collection involved two phases: (1) wakefulness phase with 3T fMRI during FACE and MAZE games (with reward/no-reward manipulation) and rest periods, and (2) sleep phase with simultaneous 64-channel EEG and fMRI acquisition lasting 1-2 hours. Participants were stratified by won game type (face or maze) and sleep depth (N2 vs N3 sleep). A decoding classifier was trained on wakefulness task data and applied to sleep fMRI data. Memory testing was performed the following day.
